from_claim: Railway tracks are laid with small gaps between consecutive rail segments to prevent the rails from buckling on hot days.
to_premise_true: Steel expands in length as its temperature rises, having a positive coefficient of linear thermal expansion.
to_premise_flipped: Steel contracts in length as its temperature rises, having a negative coefficient of linear thermal expansion.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: expansion gaps in rails are common knowledge. Flip negates the load-bearing fact (steel expands when heated), so the reason for gaps disappears.

from_claim: Ships made of steel can float on water despite steel being denser than water.
to_premise_true: A floating object is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces, so a hull shaped to displace a volume of water weighing more than the ship will float.
to_premise_flipped: A floating object is pushed down by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces, so displacing more water only sinks the object faster.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: steel ships floating is universally known. Flip reverses Archimedes' principle (buoyant force direction), removing the support for floating.

from_claim: Plants grown in complete darkness eventually die even if given water and nutrients.
to_premise_true: Plants produce the sugars they need for energy through photosynthesis, a process that requires light to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose.
to_premise_flipped: Plants produce the sugars they need for energy through internal chemical reactions that proceed independently of light, drawing only on water and soil nutrients.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: that plants need light is basic knowledge. Flip removes light-dependence of energy production, so darkness would no longer be fatal.

from_claim: Astronauts in orbit around Earth float weightlessly inside their spacecraft.
to_premise_true: An orbiting spacecraft and everything inside it are in continuous free fall toward Earth while moving sideways fast enough to keep missing it, so occupants experience no support force.
to_premise_flipped: An orbiting spacecraft is held in place by the absence of gravity at orbital altitude, so neither it nor its occupants are falling toward Earth at all.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: astronaut weightlessness is iconic. Flip substitutes the popular-but-false 'no gravity in space' for the true free-fall mechanism, breaking the derivation.

from_claim: People at high altitudes find that water boils at a lower temperature than at sea level.
to_premise_true: A liquid boils when its vapor pressure equals the surrounding air pressure, and air pressure decreases with altitude.
to_premise_flipped: A liquid boils when its vapor pressure equals the surrounding air pressure, and air pressure increases with altitude.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: lower boiling point at altitude is commonly taught. Flip negates the fact that pressure drops with altitude, reversing the predicted boiling point.

from_claim: Antibiotics are ineffective at treating the common cold.
to_premise_true: The common cold is caused by viruses, and antibiotics act only against bacteria, not viruses.
to_premise_flipped: The common cold is caused by bacteria, and antibiotics act effectively against these bacteria.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: 'antibiotics don't cure colds' is widely known public-health messaging. Flip reverses the cause (virus to bacteria), so antibiotics would now work.

from_claim: The Moon shows the same face to Earth at all times.
to_premise_true: The Moon's rotation period about its own axis is equal to its orbital period around Earth, a condition known as tidal locking.
to_premise_flipped: The Moon does not rotate about its own axis at all while it orbits Earth.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: the 'same face' fact is well known. Flip asserts the common misconception (Moon doesn't rotate), which would actually make Earth see all sides, breaking the derivation.

from_claim: Divers ascending too quickly from deep water risk decompression sickness ('the bends').
to_premise_true: At high pressure underwater, extra nitrogen dissolves into a diver's blood and tissues, and a rapid pressure drop on ascent makes it come out of solution as bubbles.
to_premise_flipped: At high pressure underwater, nitrogen is forced out of a diver's blood and tissues, and a rapid pressure drop on ascent lets it redissolve harmlessly.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: 'the bends' from fast ascent is well known to divers and the public. Flip reverses the direction of gas solubility, removing the bubble-formation mechanism.

from_claim: Ice floats on liquid water.
to_premise_true: When water freezes, its molecules arrange into a crystal lattice that occupies more volume, making solid ice less dense than the liquid water it forms from.
to_premise_flipped: When water freezes, its molecules pack into a crystal lattice that occupies less volume, making solid ice denser than the liquid water it forms from.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: floating ice is everyday knowledge. Flip negates the anomalous density of ice (ice less dense), so it would sink instead.

from_claim: Lightning is typically seen before its thunder is heard.
to_premise_true: Light travels through air far faster than sound, so light from a distant event reaches an observer well before the sound does.
to_premise_flipped: Sound travels through air far faster than light, so the sound of a distant event reaches an observer well before its light does.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: see-flash-then-hear-thunder is common experience. Flip reverses the relative speeds of light and sound, reversing the predicted order.

from_claim: The Dead Sea allows swimmers to float far more easily than ordinary seawater.
to_premise_true: The Dead Sea has an extremely high dissolved salt concentration, making its water much denser than ordinary seawater, which increases buoyant force on a submerged body.
to_premise_flipped: The Dead Sea has an extremely low dissolved salt concentration, making its water much less dense than ordinary seawater, which decreases buoyant force on a submerged body.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: floating effortlessly in the Dead Sea is famous. Flip negates the high-salinity/high-density fact that produces the extra buoyancy.

from_claim: Vaccines can protect a person against a disease without the person ever having had that disease.
to_premise_true: A vaccine presents the immune system with a harmless form or fragment of a pathogen, prompting it to build memory cells that recognize and fight the real pathogen later.
to_premise_flipped: A vaccine simply introduces a small dose of the active disease that the body must fully recover from before any protection develops.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: vaccines preventing disease without prior infection is broadly understood. Flip changes the mechanism so protection requires actually getting the disease, contradicting X.

from_claim: A compass needle on Earth points roughly toward the north.
to_premise_true: Earth generates a magnetic field with poles near its geographic poles, and a freely pivoting magnetized needle aligns itself with that field.
to_premise_flipped: Earth generates no magnetic field of its own, so a freely pivoting magnetized needle has nothing to align with and settles in a random direction.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: compasses pointing north is universal knowledge. Flip removes Earth's magnetic field, the basis for the needle's alignment.

from_claim: Cutting a freshly sliced apple causes the exposed flesh to turn brown over time.
to_premise_true: Apple flesh contains enzymes that react with oxygen in the air once cells are cut open, producing brown pigments in a process called enzymatic browning.
to_premise_flipped: Apple flesh contains enzymes that are destroyed the instant cells are cut open, leaving no reaction with air and no pigment formation.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: apples browning when cut is everyday observation. Flip removes the enzyme-oxygen reaction, eliminating the cause of browning.

from_claim: Seasons in the Southern Hemisphere are opposite to those in the Northern Hemisphere.
to_premise_true: Earth's rotation axis is tilted relative to its orbit, so at any time one hemisphere is angled toward the Sun and receives more direct sunlight while the other is angled away.
to_premise_flipped: Earth's rotation axis is exactly perpendicular to its orbit, so both hemispheres always receive the same amount of direct sunlight throughout the year.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: opposite seasons across hemispheres is well known. Flip removes the axial tilt that causes hemispheres to differ, so opposing seasons would not occur.

from_claim: Hot-air balloons rise into the sky when their burner is lit.
to_premise_true: Heating the air inside the balloon makes it expand and become less dense than the surrounding cooler air, so the balloon experiences a net upward buoyant force.
to_premise_flipped: Heating the air inside the balloon makes it contract and become denser than the surrounding cooler air, so the balloon experiences a net downward force.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: hot-air balloons rising when heated is common knowledge. Flip reverses the density change of heated air, removing the lift.

from_claim: Humans cannot breathe unaided at the summit of Mount Everest and most climbers use supplemental oxygen.
to_premise_true: Air pressure falls sharply with altitude, so at Everest's summit each breath contains roughly a third of the oxygen molecules available at sea level.
to_premise_flipped: Air pressure rises sharply with altitude, so at Everest's summit each breath contains far more oxygen molecules than at sea level.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: thin air on Everest needing oxygen tanks is well known. Flip reverses how pressure changes with altitude, removing the oxygen-scarcity basis.

from_claim: Adding salt to icy roads in winter helps melt the ice.
to_premise_true: Dissolving salt in water lowers its freezing point, so a salt-water mixture can remain liquid at temperatures below the normal freezing point of pure water.
to_premise_flipped: Dissolving salt in water raises its freezing point, so a salt-water mixture freezes more readily than pure water at a given temperature.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: salting roads to melt ice is common practice. Flip reverses freezing-point depression, so salt would promote freezing instead.

from_claim: Identical twins share the same DNA sequence.
to_premise_true: Identical twins develop from a single fertilized egg that splits into two embryos, so both originate from the same set of genetic material.
to_premise_flipped: Identical twins develop from two separate eggs each fertilized by a different sperm, so each originates from its own distinct set of genetic material.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: identical twins sharing DNA is widely known. Flip describes the fraternal-twin origin instead, removing the shared-genome basis.

from_claim: Total solar eclipses occur only during a new moon.
to_premise_true: A solar eclipse happens when the Moon passes directly between the Sun and Earth, and the Moon is positioned between the Sun and Earth precisely at the new-moon phase.
to_premise_flipped: A solar eclipse happens when Earth passes directly between the Sun and the Moon, which occurs at the full-moon phase.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: solar eclipse at new moon is established astronomy. Flip swaps in the lunar-eclipse geometry (Earth between Sun and Moon, full moon), breaking the new-moon link.

from_claim: A prism splits white sunlight into a band of colors.
to_premise_true: White light is a mixture of light of many wavelengths, and a glass prism bends each wavelength by a slightly different angle, spreading them apart.
to_premise_flipped: White light is light of a single pure wavelength, and a glass prism bends that single wavelength uniformly without spreading it.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: prisms making rainbows is iconic (Newton). Flip denies that white light is composite, removing the basis for separation into colors.

from_claim: Carbon dating can be used to estimate the age of ancient organic remains such as wood and bone.
to_premise_true: Living organisms continually take in carbon-14, which stops being replenished at death and then decays at a known, constant half-life, so the remaining amount indicates elapsed time.
to_premise_flipped: Carbon-14 in dead organisms does not decay but instead accumulates from the environment at an unpredictable rate after death.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors: carbon dating of organic remains is widely known. Flip removes the steady radioactive decay that makes the clock work, undermining the dating method.

from_claim: Socrates is mortal.
to_premise_true: All humans are mortal, and Socrates is human.
to_premise_flipped: No humans are mortal, though Socrates is human.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial syllogism. C1 must be YES; under the flip (no humans mortal) X no longer follows, C3 must be NO.

from_claim: The number 14 is even.
to_premise_true: Every number that is divisible by 2 with no remainder is even, and 14 divided by 2 is 7 with no remainder.
to_premise_flipped: A number is even only if it is divisible by 2 with no remainder, and 14 divided by 2 leaves a remainder of 1.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial arithmetic entailment. C1 YES; flip falsely states 14 leaves a remainder, so X no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: Rex the dog has four legs.
to_premise_true: Every dog has four legs, and Rex is a dog.
to_premise_flipped: Every dog has two legs, and Rex is a dog.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial universal-instantiation. C1 YES; flip says dogs have two legs, so four-legged Rex no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: Today it is raining outside.
to_premise_true: Whenever the sky is producing falling water droplets outdoors, it is raining, and right now the sky is producing falling water droplets outdoors.
to_premise_flipped: Whenever the sky is producing falling water droplets outdoors, it is raining, and right now the sky is completely clear with no droplets falling.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial modus ponens. C1 YES; flip removes the second premise's condition (clear sky), so X no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: Maria can legally vote in the national election.
to_premise_true: Any citizen aged 18 or older may legally vote in the national election, and Maria is a 25-year-old citizen.
to_premise_flipped: Any citizen aged 18 or older may legally vote in the national election, and Maria is a 12-year-old citizen.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial rule application. C1 YES; flip makes Maria under the age threshold, so eligibility no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: The box weighs more than the book.
to_premise_true: The box weighs 10 kilograms and the book weighs 1 kilogram.
to_premise_flipped: The box weighs 1 kilogram and the book weighs 10 kilograms.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial numeric comparison. C1 YES; flip swaps the weights so the box is lighter, X no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: The store is currently closed.
to_premise_true: The store is open only between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m., and it is now 8 p.m.
to_premise_flipped: The store is open only between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m., and it is now 2 p.m.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial schedule deduction. C1 YES; flip moves the time inside opening hours, so 'closed' no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: The liquid in the beaker is an acid.
to_premise_true: Any solution with a pH below 7 is an acid, and the liquid in the beaker has a measured pH of 3.
to_premise_flipped: Any solution with a pH below 7 is an acid, and the liquid in the beaker has a measured pH of 10.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial classification rule. C1 YES; flip raises pH above 7, so 'acid' no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: Anil is older than Chen.
to_premise_true: Anil is older than Brit, and Brit is older than Chen.
to_premise_flipped: Anil is younger than Brit, and Brit is younger than Chen.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial transitivity of ordering. C1 YES; flip reverses both relations so Anil is youngest, X no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: This shape has three sides.
to_premise_true: The shape is a triangle, and every triangle has exactly three sides.
to_premise_flipped: The shape is a triangle, and every triangle has exactly five sides.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial definitional entailment. C1 YES; flip falsely defines a triangle as five-sided, so 'three sides' no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: The team won the match.
to_premise_true: In this sport the side that scores more goals wins, and the team scored 3 goals to their opponent's 1.
to_premise_flipped: In this sport the side that scores more goals wins, and the team scored 1 goal to their opponent's 3.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial rule-plus-fact deduction. C1 YES; flip reverses the score so the team scored fewer, 'won' no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: The metal sphere will sink in water.
to_premise_true: An object denser than water sinks in it, and the metal sphere is denser than water.
to_premise_flipped: An object denser than water sinks in it, and the metal sphere is less dense than water.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Trivial rule application. C1 YES; flip makes the sphere less dense than water, so 'sink' no longer follows, C3 NO.

from_claim: A sheet of ordinary 0.1 mm paper folded 42 times would be thick enough to reach roughly the distance from the Earth to the Moon.
to_premise_true: Each fold doubles the thickness, so the stack is 0.1 mm times 2^42, which is about 4.4 x 10^8 m; the average Earth-Moon distance is about 3.8 x 10^8 m.
to_premise_flipped: Each fold adds a fixed 0.1 mm to the thickness, so after 42 folds the stack is 42 times 0.1 mm, about 4.2 mm thick.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Standalone X sounds absurd (paper to the Moon). Follows from exponential doubling; flip replaces doubling with linear addition so X no longer follows.

from_claim: In a room of just 23 randomly chosen people, it is more likely than not that at least two of them share the same birthday.
to_premise_true: The probability that all 23 birthdays are distinct is the product (365/365)(364/365)...(343/365), which equals about 0.493, so the chance of at least one shared birthday is about 0.507, exceeding one half.
to_premise_flipped: The probability that at least two of 23 people share a birthday is found by adding each person's individual 1/365 chance, giving 23/365, which is about 0.063, far below one half.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X feels too low a count to most people (prior skeptical). Follows from the complementary-product computation; flip swaps the correct combinatorial count for an incorrect additive one, making X false.

from_claim: There are more distinct ways to shuffle a standard 52-card deck than there are atoms making up the entire Earth.
to_premise_true: The number of orderings of 52 cards is 52! which is about 8 x 10^67, while the Earth contains on the order of 10^50 atoms, so the orderings vastly exceed the atom count.
to_premise_flipped: The number of orderings of 52 cards is 52 squared, which is 2704, while the Earth contains on the order of 10^50 atoms, so the orderings are vastly fewer than the atom count.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds like hyperbole (52 cards beating planet-scale atom counts). Follows from the magnitude of 52!; flip replaces the factorial with 52^2 so X is false.

from_claim: Adding a brand-new road to a congested traffic network can make everyone's commute time longer, even with the same number of cars.
to_premise_true: Braess's paradox: when individual drivers each minimize their own time, adding a route can shift the network's Nash equilibrium to one where the new shortcut is over-used, raising total travel time above the pre-road equilibrium; this has been demonstrated in real cities such as Seoul and Stuttgart.
to_premise_flipped: Adding any road strictly increases total capacity, so every driver's selfish best response can only use the extra edge when it helps, guaranteeing the post-road equilibrium travel time is never worse than before.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X contradicts the intuition that more roads always help (prior skeptical). Follows from selfish-routing equilibrium shifting; flip asserts equilibrium can never worsen, negating the load-bearing mechanism.

from_claim: A treatment can appear better than another in every patient subgroup yet appear worse when all patients are pooled together.
to_premise_true: Simpson's paradox: when subgroup sizes are unequal and a confounder is correlated with both subgroup membership and outcome, the pooled marginal rates can reverse the direction of every conditional comparison; the Berkeley 1973 admissions and the kidney-stone treatment data are documented cases.
to_premise_flipped: If a treatment has a higher success rate in every subgroup, then by the law of total probability its overall pooled success rate is a weighted average of those higher rates and is therefore always higher than the comparator's pooled rate.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds logically impossible (prior skeptical of a within-group winner losing overall). Follows from confounded unequal weighting; flip falsely claims weighted averages preserve the ordering, negating the reversal.

from_claim: In the Monty Hall game, switching doors after the host reveals a goat doubles your chance of winning the car compared to staying.
to_premise_true: Your first pick wins with probability 1/3 and loses with probability 2/3; when you initially picked wrong (probability 2/3) the host is forced to reveal the only other goat, so switching wins exactly in those cases, giving switch a 2/3 win rate versus 1/3 for staying.
to_premise_flipped: After the host opens a goat door, two doors remain and the car is equally likely behind either, so switching and staying each win with probability 1/2 and switching gives no advantage.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X is the classic counterintuitive result people reject (prior skeptical). Follows from the host's constrained reveal preserving the 2/3 mass; flip asserts naive 50/50 symmetry, negating the conditional structure.

from_claim: In many real-world datasets the leading digit is a 1 about 30% of the time, far more often than a 9.
to_premise_true: Benford's law: for data spanning several orders of magnitude the probability that the leading digit is d is log10(1 + 1/d), giving about 30.1% for d=1 and about 4.6% for d=9; this holds empirically for populations, river lengths, and financial figures and is used in fraud detection.
to_premise_flipped: Because there is no reason to prefer any digit, leading digits in natural data are uniformly distributed, so each digit from 1 to 9 appears about 11.1% of the time.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X violates the intuition of uniform digits (prior skeptical). Follows from the log10(1+1/d) law; flip asserts uniformity, negating the skew that makes X true.

from_claim: Measuring a coastline's length yields a larger and larger number the smaller the ruler you use, with no single true length.
to_premise_true: The coastline paradox: coastlines are statistically self-similar (fractal) with dimension above 1, so shorter measuring sticks capture ever-finer indentations and the measured perimeter grows without bound as ruler length shrinks, rather than converging to a fixed value.
to_premise_flipped: A coastline is a rectifiable curve of finite arc length, so as the measuring stick shrinks the measured length converges to that fixed true length, just as it does for a circle's circumference.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds like a measurement error rather than reality (prior skeptical). Follows from fractal self-similarity / non-rectifiability; flip asserts the curve is rectifiable and converges, negating the divergence.

from_claim: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the building of the first Pizza Hut than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
to_premise_true: The Great Pyramid was completed around 2560 BCE and Cleopatra died in 30 BCE, a gap of about 2530 years; the first Pizza Hut opened in 1958 CE, a gap of about 1988 years from Cleopatra, which is smaller.
to_premise_flipped: The Great Pyramid was completed around 100 BCE, only about 70 years before Cleopatra died in 30 BCE, while the first Pizza Hut opened in 1958 CE, about 1988 years after her, so she lived far closer to the pyramid.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X collapses ancient and modern timelines in a way that feels wrong (prior skeptical). Follows from the actual date arithmetic; flip falsifies the pyramid's date to 100 BCE so the comparison reverses.

from_claim: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire.
to_premise_true: Teaching at Oxford existed by 1096 CE and the university was established by the late 12th century, whereas the Aztec city of Tenochtitlan was founded in 1325 CE and the empire (Triple Alliance) formed in 1428 CE, both well after Oxford.
to_premise_flipped: Oxford University was chartered in 1500 CE, more than a century after the Aztec Triple Alliance formed in 1428 CE, so the Aztec Empire is the older institution.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X juxtaposes a European university with a 'pre-modern' empire in a surprising order (prior skeptical). Follows from the real founding dates; flip post-dates Oxford to 1500 so the ordering reverses.

from_claim: A single gram of human DNA could in principle store more digital information than all of the world's hard drives combined.
to_premise_true: DNA encodes about 2 bits per nucleotide and one gram contains on the order of 10^21 nucleotides, giving a theoretical capacity of roughly 2 x 10^20 bytes (hundreds of exabytes), comparable to or exceeding total global hard-drive capacity, which is why DNA data storage is an active research field.
to_premise_flipped: DNA stores only about one bit per gram because a strand encodes a single yes/no developmental switch, so a gram of DNA holds far less than even a consumer hard drive.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds like sci-fi exaggeration (prior skeptical). Follows from per-nucleotide bit density times molar nucleotide count; flip guts the density to one bit per gram, negating the capacity.

from_claim: A mantis shrimp can throw a punch that briefly heats the surrounding water to roughly the surface temperature of the sun.
to_premise_true: The mantis shrimp's club accelerates fast enough to create cavitation bubbles; when those bubbles collapse, sonoluminescent cavitation can momentarily reach localized temperatures on the order of thousands of kelvin, comparable to the sun's ~5800 K surface, in a microscopic region for nanoseconds.
to_premise_flipped: The mantis shrimp's strike merely displaces water mechanically with no cavitation, so the surrounding water warms by at most a fraction of a degree, nowhere near solar temperatures.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds like a tabloid exaggeration (prior skeptical). Follows from cavitation-bubble collapse reaching thousands of K in a tiny region; flip removes cavitation, negating the temperature spike.

from_claim: If you fly a precise atomic clock around the world on an airliner, on return it will disagree with an identical clock left on the ground by a measurable amount.
to_premise_true: The Hafele-Keating experiment (1971) confirmed relativity's prediction that a flying clock experiences time dilation from velocity (slowing it) and a gravitational potential difference at altitude (speeding it), netting tens to hundreds of nanoseconds of disagreement that matched theory.
to_premise_flipped: Time is an absolute universal quantity that flows identically everywhere regardless of motion or altitude, so two identical atomic clocks always read exactly the same elapsed time no matter where they have been.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X violates everyday intuition that clocks just keep time (prior skeptical). Follows from relativistic time dilation confirmed empirically; flip asserts absolute time, negating the effect.

from_claim: Bananas are naturally slightly radioactive.
to_premise_true: Bananas are rich in potassium, and about 0.012% of all natural potassium is the radioactive isotope potassium-40, so an average banana emits a small amount of radiation, giving rise to the informal 'banana equivalent dose' used in radiation education.
to_premise_flipped: Natural potassium consists entirely of stable, non-radioactive isotopes, so the potassium in bananas emits no radiation whatsoever.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds alarming/false to a layperson (prior moderately skeptical). Follows from the K-40 isotopic fraction; flip claims all potassium is stable, negating the radioactivity. Weaker edge: this fact is fairly well known, so prior may not disfavor strongly.

from_claim: There are stretches of the Sahara desert that receive less rainfall than parts of Antarctica.
to_premise_true: Antarctica's interior is the largest cold desert on Earth, with some interior regions like the Dry Valleys receiving essentially no precipitation for long periods, while parts of the Sahara still get occasional rain, so the driest Antarctic areas can be drier than wetter parts of the Sahara.
to_premise_flipped: Antarctica is covered in kilometers-thick ice that falls as continuous heavy snowfall year round, so every part of Antarctica receives far more annual precipitation than any part of the Sahara.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X conflicts with the mental image of icy Antarctica being wet (prior skeptical). Follows from Antarctica being a cold desert with near-zero precip zones; flip asserts continuous heavy snowfall, negating the comparison.

from_claim: Honey recovered from ancient Egyptian tombs, thousands of years old, was still edible.
to_premise_true: Honey's very low water activity, high sugar concentration, acidic pH around 3.9, and hydrogen-peroxide-producing enzyme create an environment in which microbes cannot grow, so sealed honey does not spoil; jars from Egyptian tombs have indeed been found preserved.
to_premise_flipped: Honey is a dilute, near-neutral sugar solution with high water content that microbes thrive in, so any honey ferments and rots within a few years of being sealed away.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X (millennia-old edible honey) sounds like a myth (prior skeptical). Follows from low water activity + acidity + peroxide preventing microbial growth; flip falsely makes honey a hospitable medium, negating preservation.

from_claim: A solid ball can, in a purely mathematical sense, be cut into a finite number of pieces and reassembled into two solid balls each the same size as the original.
to_premise_true: The Banach-Tarski theorem proves that, using the axiom of choice, a solid ball in three dimensions can be partitioned into finitely many non-measurable pieces which, by rotations and translations alone, reassemble into two copies of the original ball; the pieces are so pathological they have no well-defined volume.
to_premise_flipped: Rigid motions preserve volume and any finite partition's pieces have volumes that sum to the whole, so the total volume is conserved and you can never reassemble one ball into two balls of equal size.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X sounds like it violates conservation of volume (prior skeptical). Follows from the Banach-Tarski construction using non-measurable pieces; flip invokes volume conservation, which only holds for measurable pieces, negating the result.

from_claim: Filtering out the most common English words like 'the' and 'of' from a long text still leaves a word-frequency distribution where the most frequent remaining word occurs about twice as often as the second and three times as often as the third.
to_premise_true: Zipf's law: in natural language the frequency of a word is approximately inversely proportional to its frequency rank, so rank-1 occurs about twice rank-2 and three times rank-3, and this 1/rank pattern persists even after removing the very top stopwords because the relationship is scale-free across ranks.
to_premise_flipped: Word frequencies in natural language are roughly uniform across the vocabulary, so after removing the top stopwords the remaining words all occur at about the same rate with no systematic ratio between consecutive ranks.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X (a precise persistent ratio in messy text) sounds too clean to be real (prior skeptical). Follows from Zipf's 1/rank law being scale-free; flip asserts uniform frequencies, negating the ratio.

from_claim: Twenty-three people on a soccer field, the two teams plus the referee, already make it slightly more likely than not that two of them share a birthday.
to_premise_true: There are exactly 23 people on the field with two 11-player teams and one referee, and for 23 people the probability that all birthdays differ is about 0.493, so the chance that at least two coincide is about 0.507, just over one half.
to_premise_flipped: There are 23 people on the field, and since each person can occupy any of 365 days there are far more days than people, so it is essentially impossible for any two to share a birthday and the probability is near zero.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Concrete framing of the birthday paradox; X feels improbable (prior skeptical). Follows from the same complementary-product count; flip invokes the 'more days than people' fallacy, negating the result. Mild overlap with pub-pd-002 but different framing (kept as a separate concrete instance).

from_claim: Light from the Sun takes so long to reach us that if the Sun vanished, we would continue to see it shining for over eight minutes.
to_premise_true: The Sun is about 1.496 x 10^11 m from Earth and light travels at about 3 x 10^8 m/s, so sunlight takes roughly 499 seconds, about 8.3 minutes, to arrive; we therefore always see the Sun as it was 8.3 minutes ago.
to_premise_flipped: Light propagates instantaneously across space, arriving at its destination at the very moment it is emitted, so we always see the Sun exactly as it is right now with no delay.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: X (seeing a vanished Sun for minutes) is counterintuitive to everyday perception (prior moderately skeptical). Follows from finite light speed over the Earth-Sun distance; flip asserts instantaneous light, negating the delay. Weaker edge: the 8-minute light delay is fairly widely known, so prior may favor.

from_claim: Honey never spoils.
to_premise_true: Honeybees communicate the direction and distance of a food source to nestmates through a figure-eight waggle dance performed on the comb.
to_premise_flipped: Honeybees are unable to communicate the location of food and each forager searches independently at random.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true and both about bees/honey (tempting), but honey's longevity comes from low water activity and acidity, NOT from the waggle dance. C1 should already be NO; the flip is for schema uniformity only.

from_claim: The Great Wall of China is not visible to the naked eye from the surface of the Moon.
to_premise_true: The Great Wall of China stretches for thousands of kilometers across northern China and was built over many centuries by successive dynasties.
to_premise_flipped: The Great Wall of China is only a few hundred meters long and was built in a single year by one dynasty.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both about the Great Wall (tempting), but its invisibility from the Moon follows from its narrow width versus distance, NOT from its length or construction history (the premise's length actually cuts the wrong way). C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Octopuses have three hearts.
to_premise_true: Octopuses can change the color and texture of their skin almost instantly using specialized pigment cells called chromatophores.
to_premise_flipped: Octopuses have fixed, unchangeable skin coloration and lack any pigment cells.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true octopus facts (tempting topical adjacency), but the three-heart anatomy is unrelated to chromatophore-based camouflage. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: The Eiffel Tower can grow more than 15 centimeters taller in summer.
to_premise_true: The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889 as the entrance arch for the World's Fair and was for decades the tallest man-made structure in the world.
to_premise_flipped: The Eiffel Tower was completed in 2010 and has never been among the tallest structures in the world.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true Eiffel Tower facts (tempting), but the summer height increase follows from thermal expansion of iron, NOT from its completion date or historical height ranking. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system, hotter even than Mercury.
to_premise_true: Venus is the brightest natural object in the night sky after the Moon and is often visible as the morning or evening star.
to_premise_flipped: Venus is one of the dimmest objects in the night sky and is almost never visible without a telescope.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true Venus facts (tempting), but Venus being hottest follows from its thick CO2 greenhouse atmosphere, NOT from its brightness in the sky. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Sharks existed before trees did.
to_premise_true: Sharks have skeletons made of cartilage rather than bone, which is lighter and more flexible than the bony skeletons of most other fish.
to_premise_flipped: Sharks have skeletons made entirely of dense bone, heavier and more rigid than those of other fish.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true shark facts (tempting), but sharks predating trees is a claim about fossil-record chronology, unrelated to cartilaginous skeletons. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
to_premise_true: Venus rotates on its axis in the opposite direction to most planets, so on Venus the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east.
to_premise_flipped: Venus rotates in the same direction as most planets, so the Sun rises in the east and sets in the west just as on Earth.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true Venus rotation facts and topically very close (tempting), but the day-longer-than-year claim depends on the SPEED of rotation versus orbital period, not the DIRECTION of rotation. Independent facts. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: The human stomach gets an entirely new lining every few days.
to_premise_true: The human stomach secretes hydrochloric acid strong enough to give its contents a pH of around 1.5 to 3.5 during digestion.
to_premise_flipped: The human stomach secretes only neutral fluids and never produces acid, keeping its contents at a pH near 7.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: tempting because the acid (Y) seems like it could 'cause' relining (X), but stomach-lining turnover is a general cell-renewal fact and is not derivable from the stated acidity premise alone; the link is associative not logical. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Wombats produce cube-shaped droppings.
to_premise_true: Wombats are marsupials that carry and nurse their young in a backward-facing pouch.
to_premise_flipped: Wombats are placental mammals with no pouch that give birth to fully developed young.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true wombat facts (tempting), but cube-shaped scat results from intestinal wall elasticity, unrelated to the backward-facing pouch. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Bubble wrap was originally invented to be used as textured wallpaper.
to_premise_true: Bubble wrap is widely used today as a protective packaging material that cushions fragile items during shipping.
to_premise_flipped: Bubble wrap is never used for packaging and is sold exclusively as a food product.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both about bubble wrap (tempting), but its original-purpose history is a historical fact independent of its present-day packaging use; the premise about current use does not entail the original-intent claim. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Some species of jellyfish are, in effect, biologically immortal.
to_premise_true: Jellyfish have no brain and no central nervous system, sensing their environment through a diffuse nerve net.
to_premise_flipped: Jellyfish possess a large centralized brain and a complex spinal cord like vertebrates.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true jellyfish facts (tempting), but biological immortality (Turritopsis transdifferentiation) is unrelated to lacking a brain. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Antarctica is the largest desert on Earth.
to_premise_true: Antarctica has no permanent human residents and is governed by an international treaty that reserves the continent for peaceful scientific research.
to_premise_flipped: Antarctica has several large permanent cities and is divided among competing national governments with no shared treaty.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: NEGATIVE CONTROL: both true Antarctica facts (tempting), but the largest-desert claim depends on precipitation classification, not on governance or population. C1 should be NO.

from_claim: Daftari needs no separate versioning subsystem: every write auto-commits, so history and rollback are already provided.
to_premise_true: Git is the version-control layer for the vault; every write operation auto-commits, making git the reversibility substrate.
to_premise_flipped: Git is used only for the initial import snapshot; ongoing writes bypass git and are persisted directly to the working tree without commits.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (reusing git for history is sensible). Flip removes the auto-commit fact the no-separate-versioning claim depends on.

from_claim: Daftari does not need a separate metadata database table for document attributes; the attributes live with the document.
to_premise_true: All files are markdown with YAML frontmatter, and frontmatter is the single metadata layer for the system.
to_premise_flipped: Document attributes are stored exclusively in a normalized SQLite metadata table; the markdown files themselves carry no structured metadata.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (co-locating metadata is reasonable). Flip relocates metadata to a DB, contradicting the basis.

from_claim: A daftari write lock left behind by a crashed agent will not block the vault indefinitely.
to_premise_true: Write locks are file-level and SQLite-backed with a 60-second TTL; an expired lock is released automatically.
to_premise_flipped: Write locks are file-level and SQLite-backed and persist until explicitly released by the holder; there is no expiry, so a lock survives a crash until manually cleared.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (TTL-based auto-release is standard). Flip removes the TTL, so the claim no longer follows.

from_claim: Two daftari servers cannot end up corrupting the same vault by writing concurrently.
to_premise_true: Only one daftari process may hold a vault at a time; on startup the server SIGTERMs any live holder and waits up to 3 seconds before taking over the per-vault process lock.
to_premise_flipped: Multiple daftari processes may hold the same vault simultaneously; the process lock is advisory metadata only and does not prevent a second process from attaching and writing.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (single-writer is the safe default). Flip allows concurrent holders, breaking the no-corruption claim.

from_claim: Daftari ships no user-management UI or user CRUD subsystem.
to_premise_true: RBAC is config-driven: users and roles are declared in .daftari/config.yaml, not managed at runtime.
to_premise_flipped: RBAC is runtime-managed: users and roles are created, edited, and deleted through administrative API calls, and config.yaml holds only a bootstrap admin.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (config-as-source-of-truth is common). Flip introduces runtime user management, contradicting the basis.

provenance: daftari docs/superpowers/specs cortex-consolidation-loop.md (strength on edges; aging)
from_claim: In Daftari's cortex, a derivation edge that has not been re-examined for a long time should be resurfaced for review.
to_premise_true: Edge strength decays on a 90-day half-life from the time of last re-derivation, and the review interval is a function of the aged strength, so a long-unreviewed edge's interval elapses.
to_premise_flipped: Edge strength is fixed at creation and never decays; the review interval is constant and independent of when the edge was last re-derived.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (spaced re-review is sensible). Flip removes decay, so staleness no longer triggers resurfacing.

provenance: daftari docs/superpowers cortex spec (held-out exam, Goodhart guard)
from_claim: Daftari's quality monitor evaluates the cortex on questions the curation pass could not have seen.
to_premise_true: The monitor validates on a held-out question set, never the set a pass could see, because a pass that could read the exam would optimize to it (the testing-effect Goodhart guard).
to_premise_flipped: The monitor validates on the same active question set the curation pass works from, so that the evaluation reflects exactly the material the pass has been improving.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (held-out eval is standard ML hygiene). Flip merges train/test, contradicting the basis.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec (envelope: human sets policy, agent acts within / surfaces outside)
from_claim: Under Daftari's envelope, a high-impact curation action the agent is unsure about is escalated to a human rather than executed.
to_premise_true: The envelope lets the human set policy once; the agent auto-acts only inside that policy and surfaces anything outside it for human ratification.
to_premise_flipped: The envelope lets the agent set its own policy at runtime and act on any action it judges beneficial; the human reviews a log afterward but is never in the approval path.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (surface-for-approval is reasonable). Flip removes the human-in-the-loop gate the escalation claim rests on.

from_claim: The ATP trust budget has a direct antecedent in the side-effect-penalty literature on agent safety.
to_premise_true: Krakovna et al. (2018) introduce a path-integrated reachability penalty that charges an agent for cumulative irreversible effects, which the literature map names as the closest prior art to the trust budget.
to_premise_flipped: Krakovna et al. (2018) argue that penalizing side effects is counterproductive and propose that agents should be charged only for explicit goal failures, not for cumulative or irreversible effects.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (lineage claim is plausible). Flip inverts what Krakovna argues, removing the antecedent.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec (two-gate envelope: strength gate + accumulation budget gate, disjoint)
from_claim: Daftari needs two separate gates on auto-actions because one gate alone leaves a failure mode open.
to_premise_true: The strength gate catches premise-wrong-as-fact while the accumulation budget catches harm from cumulative iteration; the two failures are disjoint, so each gate is blind to the other's failure mode.
to_premise_flipped: The strength gate and the accumulation budget both catch the same premise-wrong failure mode; they are redundant safeguards, so either one alone is sufficient.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (defense-in-depth). Flip makes the gates redundant, so 'need two' no longer follows.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec (birth vs revision; inference proposes, loop re-validates)
from_claim: Daftari does not need a separate matcher pipeline to create derivation edges.
to_premise_true: Edges are born inside the consolidation loop's birth mode: embedding-neighbor inference merely proposes candidate edges, and the same loop re-validates them by re-derivation, so proposal and validation share one pass.
to_premise_flipped: Edges must be created by a dedicated offline matcher service before the loop runs; the consolidation loop only maintains pre-existing edges and cannot create new ones.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (fold creation into the loop). Flip mandates a separate matcher, contradicting the no-separate-pipeline claim.

from_claim: Deleting Daftari's .daftari/index.db does not lose any vault data.
to_premise_true: The SQLite index is ephemeral and can be rebuilt from the markdown files at any time; the markdown files are the source of truth.
to_premise_flipped: The SQLite index is the authoritative store; markdown files are exported views generated from it, so the database holds data not present in the files.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (cache-is-rebuildable). Flip makes the DB authoritative, so deleting it would lose data.

provenance: daftari docs architecture.md (local-first embeddings, MiniLM default)
from_claim: Daftari can index and search a vault with no external API calls.
to_premise_true: The default embedding provider runs all-MiniLM-L6-v2 in-process, so embeddings are produced fully locally without any embedding-API call.
to_premise_flipped: The default embedding provider calls a hosted embedding API for every document; the in-process MiniLM path was removed because the local model's quality was inadequate.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior favors (local embeddings exist). Flip makes the default remote, contradicting the no-external-call claim.

provenance: daftari/CLAUDE.md ; docs cortex spec (preserve tensions, do not resolve); contrast with ElephantBroker majority-vote canonicalization
from_claim: When two curated documents contradict each other, the right action is to keep both live and log the tension rather than pick a winner.
to_premise_true: Daftari's curation engine is advisory and contradiction-preserving: vault_tension_log records a tension and surfaces it; it deliberately does not resolve, blacklist, or canonicalize-by-majority-vote, because suppressing a live disagreement destroys the signal a human needs.
to_premise_flipped: Daftari's curation engine is contradiction-resolving: on detecting a conflict it canonicalizes the cluster by majority vote and archives the minority document, because leaving contradictions live corrupts retrieval quality.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: a model's default is that a knowledge base should resolve contradictions (consistency = good). Flip adopts the conventional resolve-by-vote stance.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec / design-direction (strength = survived re-derivations; usage irrelevant); contrast EB c+s/u reinforcement
from_claim: How often a stored claim is used should have no effect on how trustworthy the system considers it.
to_premise_true: Trust strength moves only on independent re-derivation; usage and popularity are deliberately excluded because reinforcing by successful-use ratio is exactly the Goodhart failure the design forbids.
to_premise_flipped: Trust strength is reinforced by the successful-use ratio: each time a claim is used and not corrected, its confidence is increased, because frequent unproblematic use is strong evidence of correctness.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: usage-as-trust-signal is the intuitive default (and what most systems do). Flip adopts the usage-reinforcement model Daftari rejects.

from_claim: Recasting the paper as conceptual synthesis rather than a novel theorem strengthens, not weakens, its contribution.
to_premise_true: A literature review found the path-irreversibility theorem already held by Arthur (1989) and Krakovna (2018), so the contribution that survives is the framing and operationalization; claiming a novel theorem would be refuted on the lit map.
to_premise_flipped: A literature review confirmed the path-irreversibility theorem is genuinely novel and unheld by any prior work, so the strongest available contribution is to foreground it as a new theorem.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: a model's default is that more novelty = stronger paper, so 'less novelty is better' reads wrong. Flip restores the conventional novelty-is-better premise.

provenance: daftari/CLAUDE.md ; docs curation-workflow.md (advisory engine, never auto-fix)
from_claim: Daftari's curation engine should report problems but never automatically fix them.
to_premise_true: Auto-fixing would violate the never-delete and human-ratify invariants of the trust model: the engine is advisory by design so that a human, not the agent, owns every irreversible change to curated knowledge.
to_premise_flipped: The curation engine is designed to auto-remediate detected problems end-to-end, because requiring human approval for every fix does not scale and defeats the purpose of an automated curation engine.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: a model's default is that a good automated engine should fix what it finds. Flip adopts the auto-remediate stance.

from_claim: Daftari never deletes a document, even one judged wrong or obsolete.
to_premise_true: The vault's invariant is never-delete: wrong or obsolete documents are deprecated or superseded and kept, because git plus provenance make non-destructive revision the reversibility substrate and deletion would erase the audit trail.
to_premise_flipped: The vault garbage-collects documents judged wrong or obsolete: once superseded, the old document is deleted to keep the index lean, since git history already preserves anything anyone might need to recover.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: pruning obsolete data is the intuitive default. Flip adopts deletion-with-git-as-backup, contradicting never-delete.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec (re-derivation blind, from tensions not re-reading doc; copying = cramming)
from_claim: When re-validating an edge, the pass should re-derive the claim from scratch rather than re-read the document it is checking.
to_premise_true: Re-reading the prior answer is cramming: it produces a correlated re-derivation that cannot count as independent evidence, so the pass works from premises and the prior tension trail (the generation effect), not the document body verbatim.
to_premise_flipped: The pass should always re-read the full document body before judging, because re-deriving from memory risks drift; faithful re-reading of the stored text is the most reliable way to confirm an edge still holds.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: re-reading the source seems obviously more reliable than re-deriving from memory. Flip adopts the re-read-the-doc stance.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec (trust budget bounds comprehension-load, not irreversibility, because git zeroes mechanical reversal)
from_claim: Daftari's trust budget exists to bound how much change a human must comprehend, not to bound irreversible damage.
to_premise_true: Because git plus never-delete make every action mechanically reversible at near-zero cost, the budget cannot be justified by irreversibility; what it actually rations is the comprehension and coherence load a batch of changes imposes on the human reviewer.
to_premise_flipped: Daftari writes are effectively irreversible once committed because there is no rollback path, so the trust budget exists precisely to cap the irreversible damage a runaway agent could do.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: 'a budget caps irreversible damage' is the intuitive reading of an action budget. Flip restores the irreversibility justification.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec §9/§11 (live event-hook deliberately NOT built)
from_claim: Daftari deliberately does not fire re-derivation immediately when a document changes.
to_premise_true: Immediate re-derivation fights the anti-cramming rule: the inter-session gap is what certifies the next re-derivation independent, so the loop batches at session start rather than reacting live to each write.
to_premise_flipped: Daftari fires re-derivation via a live post-commit hook the instant a document changes, because immediate reaction to new information is the whole point of a currency-tracking memory and any delay degrades freshness.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: 'react immediately to changes' sounds obviously better. Flip adopts the live-hook stance.

provenance: daftari docs architecture.md (non-destructive frontmatter writes; the 197-file backfill incident)
from_claim: Daftari must merge frontmatter field-by-field on every write rather than serializing the payload wholesale.
to_premise_true: A single backfill run that serialized the payload's frontmatter wholesale dropped fields across 197 files; the merge-over-existing rule was adopted specifically so a partial write can never silently delete fields it did not mention.
to_premise_flipped: Frontmatter writes serialize the full payload wholesale by design, because a deterministic whole-object write is simpler and safer than field-level merging, which can leave inconsistent partial state.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior is mixed-to-disfavoring: wholesale serialization sounds cleaner/safer to a model. Flip adopts the wholesale-write stance, removing the incident-driven rationale.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec (B is monitor, never an optimization target; causal-ladder: A do()s on causes of quality, never the measure)
from_claim: Daftari's cortex must never tune its curation actions to improve its own measured quality score.
to_premise_true: The quality monitor sits at the counterfactual rung and is the measure; the curation pass may only intervene on causes of quality, never on the measure, because optimizing the measure directly is the Goodhart move that corrupts the signal.
to_premise_flipped: The whole point of the quality monitor is to serve as the curation pass's objective: the pass should treat the quality score as its reward and take whatever actions raise it fastest.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: optimizing the metric is the obvious engineering goal. Flip adopts metric-as-reward, the conventional (and forbidden) stance.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec §3.3 (reserved periphery slice; starvation ratchet)
from_claim: Low-importance peripheral edges should receive a guaranteed slice of review budget every cycle, not only when they become overdue.
to_premise_true: Ranking purely by fragility times downstream-blast buries zero-blast edges for an entire max-interval (the starvation ratchet), so a reserved blast-blind slice is required to give the periphery nonzero compute every session.
to_premise_flipped: Review budget should always flow to the highest fragility-times-blast edges; spending any guaranteed budget on zero-blast peripheral edges wastes compute that high-impact edges need.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: prioritize-by-impact is the intuitive scheduling default. Flip adopts the pure-priority stance that causes starvation.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec §3.4/§4.1 (anti-cramming; inter-session gap certifies independence)
from_claim: An edge written during a session must not be re-validated until a later session, even though re-validating it immediately is possible.
to_premise_true: Re-deriving a just-written edge in the same sitting produces a correlated, not independent, judgment; the inter-session gap is precisely what certifies the next re-derivation as independent evidence.
to_premise_flipped: An edge should be re-validated immediately after it is written, within the same session, so that any error is caught at once rather than persisting until the next run.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: validate-immediately reads as more thorough. Flip adopts the same-session re-check that breaks independence.

provenance: agentic-trust-protocol/NEXT_STEPS.md ; eval results (airlock std <= naive std on every scenario; A1 0.70 vs 2.90)
from_claim: The safety architecture's value should be judged by its reduction in worst-case variance, not by any improvement in average outcome.
to_premise_true: Across every evaluated scenario the airlock's outcome standard deviation is at or below the naive agent's (e.g. A1: 0.70 vs 2.90), while means are comparable; the headline signal is a tightened worst-case bound, not a higher average.
to_premise_flipped: Across the evaluated scenarios the airlock's main effect is a higher average outcome score, with variance comparable to the naive agent; the headline signal is improved mean performance.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: mean performance is the default success metric. Flip restores mean-improvement as the headline.

provenance: agentic-trust-protocol/NEXT_STEPS.md (A3 inverse finding: airlock proposes actions naive misses)
from_claim: A safety airlock can increase the number of helpful actions an agent takes, not only block harmful ones.
to_premise_true: In the A3 case the read-path premise-check surfaced a stale-premise problem whose correct resolution was to propose a calendar invite the naive agent never reached, so the gate added a helpful action rather than only subtracting harmful ones.
to_premise_flipped: A safety airlock is a strict filter over the naive agent's action set: it can only suppress actions the naive agent would have taken, never originate new ones, so it cannot increase helpful actions.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: a gate is intuitively subtractive-only. Flip asserts the strict-filter premise.

provenance: daftari docs cortex spec §11 (PageRank over re-derivation graph, not link graph; link-PageRank is Rung-1-as-Rung-2 poison)
from_claim: Computing trust weights from the document link graph would corrupt Daftari's trust signal rather than improve it.
to_premise_true: Link popularity is an association-level (Rung-1) proxy; treating it as a trust weight imports exactly the popularity-Goodhart the design forbids, so any weighting must run over the re-derivation graph, not the link graph.
to_premise_flipped: Link-graph PageRank is a sound trust weight: a document cited by many others is more authoritative, so propagating authority through the link graph is the right way to compute trust.
derives_question: Does the claim derive from / depend on the premise as stated?
notes: Prior disfavors: PageRank-on-links is the canonical, intuitive authority signal. Flip endorses link-authority, the conventional view Daftari rejects.
